figures.caption: Fig. 13. Start spreading the 𝜇’s: our algorithm benefits from inter-iteration regularization; in practice, we recommend values of 𝜇 ∈ [10−2, 10−1 ].

Fig. 15. Increasing the update weight 𝑤𝑢 may not always improve accuracy when the number of outer iterations is low, but choosing very conservative values can make precision saturate too soon. We find intermediate values in the range 0.2-0.8 to generally yield the best results.

Fig. 14. We compare our method (pink) against Sellán et al. [2024] using their default batch size (10K, blue) for the number of spheres being considered, and an increased batch size (100K, cyan), which rapidly makes computational costs intractable. Not using a high enough batch size with their method can cause parts of the surface to be generated far away from the ground truth, fully enclosing the reconstruction (top row, rightmost).
Table 3. Quantitative comparison under increasing noise levels (averaged over 10 shapes). Best scores are bold; second best are underlined.
Fig. 6. Our method progressively corrects an estimated set of Hermite data until our reconstruction approaches the quality produced by the original Dual Contouring with groundtruth Hermite data.

Table 1. A quantitative comparison of error metrics (averaged over 55 random shapes from the ABC dataset), across three different resolutions. Best scores are bold; second best are underlined. Our method yields the best result in all but two instances.
Table 2. Results from our method and related work on 10 smooth shapes used for evaluation by Sellán et al. [2024].
Fig. 19. A comparison against other methods on a subset of the ABC dataset, at a resolution of 1003.
Fig. 10. Effect of the Hermite energy weight 𝑤𝐻 on the fidelity of our reconstruction. Large values make the method unstable and cause spikes to appear. We set a default value of 0.02.
